title: Wovor warnt dich niemand bei der Gründung eines Unternehmens?
Most micro-SaaS founders massively underestimate how much downtime actually costs them
body:
I spent some time researching how much it really costs micro-SaaS companies when their application(s) go down. As solo founders and indie hackers, we get excited about launching our product and spend little to no time putting in place adequate uptime monitoring tools to avoid prolonged outages. If your customer is the one alerting you that your app is down, you already lost.

You must factor in customer churn, the impact on brand reputation, and, if you have grown enough to have employees, the loss of trust within your own organization. I created a calculator (available online) that estimates the total outage costs by the hour. You won’t believe the figures!

A two-hour outage can cost $530.00 for a Saas generating 10k MRR

title: ops management needs a refresh....
body:
Idea I'm exploring: operations support built specifically for small service businesses

Background on me — I spent years as an account manager in advertising, owning clients, projects, scope, onboarding, and financials. Then I started my own product business in 2021 and even with all that experience, finding talent who could actually move the needle operationally was exhausting. I eventually had to step back.

While I was out, I kept seeing founders like Ami Cole and Hanifa in the conversation — incredible operators doing creative work but clearly overwhelmed by the backend of scaling. And I kept thinking: the gap isn't hustle or vision. It's ops.

Here's what I keep observing with small service businesses (under 10 people):

\- Affordable ops talent exists but it's mostly overseas, backend-only, and can't be client-facing

\- So things like onboarding, scope creep, proposals, and client communication fall back on the founder

\- The founder becomes the bottleneck — and growth stalls

What I'm exploring is a service that plugs directly into a founder's business as a trusted ops partner. Not a VA. Not a project management course. Something that actually owns the operational layer so the founder can build.

I'm still in validation mode — currently running a short survey to hear from founders directly before I build anything.

Curious if anyone here has seen this problem up close or has thoughts on the idea. What am I missing?

title: Never email yourself a file again
body:
You're at work and suddenly need a file from your home computer.  
Instead of emailing it to yourself, uploading it to the cloud, or setting up remote access, you just open a browser and download it.

The idea is simple:

You install a small app on your home computer and sign in. The app runs quietly in the background and securely connects your machine to a simple web interface.

Whenever you need something, you log in from any browser and see the files on that computer. You can browse folders and download whatever you need.

No port forwarding.  
No router configuration.  
No FTP servers.  
No syncing folders to the cloud.

Your computer basically becomes **your personal cloud**.

Most of the time we don’t actually need all our files everywhere. We just occasionally need access to something that’s sitting on another machine.

Cloud storage forces you to upload everything in advance “just in case.”  
Setting up your own server or remote access is too technical for most people.

This idea sits in the middle:

* easier than running your own server
* no uploading or syncing like cloud storage
* just install the app and access your files when you need them

I’m curious what people think.

Would you use something like this?

What obvious problems am I missing?

title: I built a platform where founders get discovered by showing what they built, not sending cold emails into the void
body:
YC says your first launch should never be your only launch. Most founders treat launching like a one-time event. You post on Product Hunt, maybe get some upvotes, and then what? Back to being invisible.

That's the problem I'm solving with FirstLookk.

It's a video-first discovery platform for early stage founders. Instead of sending 40-page pitch decks into inboxes that never open them, you record a short demo of what you're building. Real conviction. Real product. Real you. Investors, early adopters, and the community scroll through and discover founders based on merit, not warm intros.

The whole idea is simple. If what you built is good, people should be able to find it. Right now they can't. Discovery is still a network game and most founders don't have one yet.

FirstLookk is meant to be a launchpad you can come back to. Ship an update, post a new demo. Build traction over time instead of betting everything on a single launch day that disappears in 24 hours.

We're onboarding founding users right now. If you're building something and nobody knows about it yet, that's exactly who this is for.

[firstlookk.com](http://firstlookk.com)

Would love feedback from this community. What would make you actually post your product on a platform like this?

title: 19 with ADHD – Built my own dumb quiz-lock app to block Reddit until I study. It's actually working lol
body:
This sem has been actual hell. skipping class, assignments piling up, sitting in front of my laptop for 4 hours straight and accomplishing jack shit because i keep opening my phone. reddit was the biggest problem. i’d tell myself “just 5 mins” and next thing i know it’s 1am, i got a quiz in 7 hours, and i just read 47 posts about someone’s dog stealing pizza.

i’m flat broke. can’t buy those premium focus apps or pay for a tutor. it’s just me vs my trash self-control.

so i got fed up and made my own stupid little thing. basically an app that straight up blocks reddit (and insta, tiktok whatever) until i answer a quick quiz. i put in questions from my own note physics formulas, chem reactions, whatever i’m supposed to be reviewing.

first time i tried to open reddit it hit me with a question and i was like “you’ve gotta be kidding me” out loud. alone in my room. felt like an idiot.

but i answered it. got it right. reddit unlocked. and i was like wait… i just reviewed that topic without even trying.

now every time the urge hits to scroll, my brain goes “fine fine i’ll do the quiz” because it wants the dopamine so bad. it’s tricked itself into studying lmao.

i’m not saying i’m suddenly a genius. still failing some stuff, still messy. but i’m actually passing classes i was 100% gonna bomb. that’s more than i had going last month.

if you’re broke, scrolling 24/7, and hate yourself for it sometimes you just gotta lock your own ass out. built this with basic stuff i already had, nothing fancy. works like a charm (for me at least).

anyone else made their own blocker or gamified their phone? does it actually stick or am i just weird? if you want the super basic way i set it up hit me up i’ll explain.

[Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.smilefox.app&hl=en)  
[iOS](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/smiloo-app-blocker-focus/id6756212740)

title: Day 210. Just hit 300 paid users. It still feels unreal.
body:
About 7 months ago I launched [my tool](http://leadverse.ai). Its a tool that monitors Reddit and X for people looking for something you offer and automate the DM outreach to bring your product in front of the right people automatically, book calls etc..

Few days after launch the #1 customer came in and my hands were shaking :D.

Fast forward to today and I just watched user #300 come through and it still feels unreal.. every single one of those is a real person who looked at what I built and decided yeah this is worth paying for.. that never gets old.

It's not like "I made it" or "I can retire now".. but the feeling of building something that actually helps people and getting the positive feedback is really what keeps me pushing.

Biggest thing I learned between user 1 and 300 is that the product I shipped on day 1 is almost unrecognizable now. I just kept listening to users and shipping stuff.

The other thing is that distribution is genuinely harder than building. Getting it in front of the right people every day is the actual work. Also there were weeks where growth completely stopped and I thought about quitting :)

If you're early and hearing silence just keep going. The first paying user changes your psychology.

Also, here's the [proof](https://trustmrr.com/startup/leadverse) :)

title: Most founders are still building solutions for problems that don't exist
body:
It’s 2026 and the barrier to building a SaaS is basically zero. That sounds like a dream, but it’s actually a nightmare because it means the market is getting flooded with high-quality junk. 

I’ve spent the last few months looking at why some solo founders are still moving at 10x the speed of everyone else while the rest of us are stuck in 'research' loops. The difference isn't how fast they code, it's how fast they stop building things nobody wants.

Here’s what I’ve noticed about the current founder stack:

Validation is the new velocity. If you’re spending more than 48 hours researching a niche before getting a 'go/no-go' signal from real-world data, you’re already behind. 

Decision intelligence over generative fluff. Using AI to write your landing page copy is fine, but using it to cross-reference Reddit pain points against actual search intent is how you actually find a gap.

The 'AI Tourist' trap. Most people are just playing with new wrappers on Product Hunt. The founders actually making money are using 2-3 specific tools to automate the grunt work (competitor analysis, sentiment tracking) so they can spend 90% of their time talking to users.

I used to spend weeks on 'market research' that was basically just me looking at pretty charts. Now, if I can't prove someone is actively complaining about a problem on social media within 60 seconds, I kill the idea and move on.

Curious—how are you guys vetting ideas these days? Are you still doing manual competitor deep-dives or have you automated that part of the headache yet?

Originally posted here: https://ideatolaunch.co/blog/10-essential-ai-tools-for-founders-in-2026-mmadnmy0

title: The 48-hour customer discovery sprint: how I validate ideas before writing a line of code
body:
After killing two products that had zero paying users, I started using a brutal validation filter:

Day 1: Find 5 strangers (not friends) who have the problem. Cold DM, LinkedIn, subreddits. If you can't find 5 people who clearly have this problem, stop.

Day 2: Ask each of them "What have you tried to solve this?" If they say "nothing," the pain isn't real enough. If they describe cobbled-together workarounds, you might have something.

Bonus signal: if 2 of 5 ask "when can I pay for this?" without you prompting, keep going.

Has anyone else found a faster or more reliable filter? Curious what's worked.

title: How I find micro-SaaS ideas using triggers + 80/20 gaps (instead of “scratch your own itch”)
body:
Most SaaS idea advice boils down to “scratch your own itch.” Useful, but not repeatable if you don’t already have a burning problem.

I’m a software engineer and I’ve built a few things on the side — this is the framework I keep coming back to.

Here’s the process:

1. **Look for TRIGGERS** (why now) The best opportunities often start with a trigger: a pricing shock, new limits, a pricing-model change, or a policy change that forces users to re-evaluate right now.
2. **Find the 80/20 GAP** (what to build) I look for “suite” products where most users only rely on 1–2 core workflows, but pay for everything. The play isn’t “build a better X” — it’s “build the focused version for the users who only need the core.”
3. **Spot ENTERPRISE DRIFT** Some tools are built for enterprise (complex onboarding, compliance, heavy workflows) but end up being used by freelancers/small teams who don’t need 90% of that complexity — yet pay the same.
4. **Extract the REAL job from Reddit + low-star reviews** Search Reddit: “cancel \[tool\]”, “alternatives to \[tool\]”, “\[tool\] too expensive”. Then cluster what people say into: - the one feature they truly use - what broke trust (pricing, limits, support) - what they’d pay for a simpler alternative Low-star reviews help confirm the same pain points outside Reddit. From that you can usually write a 1-sentence JTBD + a “minimum viable alternative” feature list.
5. **Look for REVENUE PROOF** (does money already exist?) Before committing, I look for evidence people are already paying in adjacent spaces (Indie Hackers revenue posts, public pricing pages, “I hit $X MRR” threads). If multiple builders are making real money nearby, the market is likely real.
6. **Sanity-check APIs + unit economics** (so you don’t build something that can’t profit) Before getting excited: can the core workflow be built on existing APIs, and do margins still work at micro-SaaS pricing after variable costs + support risk?

Quick scorecard (0–2 each, max 12 = 6 signals × 2):  
Trigger, core workflow clarity, 80/20 gap, SMB mismatch, API readiness, revenue proof.  
10–12 = test pricing today; 7–9 = research more; ≤6 = skip.

Full disclosure: I built [**MicroGaps**](https://www.microgaps.com/?utm_source=reddit&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=launch) to package this into full research reports (competitors + pricing, demand signals, build blueprint, GTM, etc.). We publish a new opportunity daily, and the free set includes 10 full reports that rotate weekly (**10 new free reports every week**).

title: Startup idea I'm actually building: what if users earned a quarterly dividend from their own purchase data?
body:
Every time you buy something you generate data — what you bought, where, when, how much. That data flows to the company that sold to you, the payment processor, the credit card company. They all profit from it. You get nothing.

I kept thinking about the Facebook model. Facebook makes roughly $40/user/year off your data through advertising. You see none of that. What if a platform said "we're going to pay users a quarterly dividend based on the aggregate value of their data"? Would people use it more? Would they share more accurate data knowing it had a direct financial return for them?

I think the answer is yes. And I think it creates a flywheel. Users are incentivized to participate and share accurate data. Accurate data is more valuable to brands. More brand revenue increases the dividend. Higher dividends attract more users. Repeat.

So I'm building it. The startup is called KACHNG. The wedge is digital receipts.

Receipts are the only part of the transaction layer that hasn't been digitized. Payments are digital, banking is digital, invoicing is digital. The receipt? Still a crumpled piece of paper you lose or an email you never open. 

That's the entry point. Here's the value stack I'm building on top of it:

Layer 1 (live now): $100 weekly sweepstakes — every receipt is an entry. This is the acquisition hook to get people in the door.

Layer 2 (next month): Cashback on purchases processed through KACHNG.

Layer 3 (building toward): Quarterly dividend based on aggregated, anonymized purchase data that brands pay to access.

Layer 4 (long term): Enterprise expense reporting — compete with SAP Concur and Expensify using the same receipt infrastructure.

Where I'm at right now: Solo founder, bootstrapping on a $500-1000/month burn. Just launched on the iOS App Store. Fewer than 50 users. 973 receipts processed. First $100 winner gets drawn Sunday. AI-powered extraction processes receipts in seconds. 

The bet is that once people have all their receipts in one place, they'll realize why this hasnt done before. And as I layer on cashback and dividends, the value of each receipt grows — making it increasingly irrational to not use KACHNG, it becomes IRRESPONSIBLE. 

Curious what this community thinks about the model. Poke holes in it, tell me what I'm missing, or tell me if you'd use it.

[https://apps.apple.com/us/app/kachng-digital-receipt/id6502332854](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/kachng-digital-receipt/id6502332854)

Stop wasting $10K building an app no one wants
body:
Let me repeat myself again, if you're spending before you've tested there's interest, you may as well throw your money down the drain.

I've seen so many founders guilty of this (even ones that have an existing successful business), they think of this 'genius' sounding idea, then pour all their heart and money into it. Even worse, they spend 2+ years developing it. And at the end... Nothing. 

Here's how to save yourself 23.5 months and $9900:

 \- Make the best MVP you physically can in a week ([this](https://gapfinderai.com/) is an example for context)

 \- Spend the next week posting about it online all day long; Reddit, LinkedIn, X, Insta, TikTok... get it out there!

 \- Measure the % of people that pay

 \- if low -> you saved yourself a long time

 \- if high -> you just got customers nearly 2 years early

Simple, yet so many people can't accept that what they think is great might not be...

title: Automatic Quote Automation Research
Unpopular opinion: Your 40-page business plan is actually a form of procrastination
body:
I've seen so many founders (myself included) spend three weeks obsessing over a SWOT analysis and formatting a Google Doc before they've even talked to a single potential customer. It feels like work, but it's really just a way to avoid the terrifying reality of the market. 

By the time you finish a 'comprehensive' plan, the market has already moved. A static document is obsolete the second you hit save. 

Here is what I've learned about planning while actually trying to build something that survives:

Drafting is a liability, iterating is an asset. If you can't summarize your unit economics and distribution strategy on one page, you don't have a business, you have a hobby.

The math usually lies. Most founders 'massage' their projections to look good for a bank loan that isn't coming. If your subscription model needs a 90% conversion rate to break even, you need to know that on day one, not month six.

Validation > Documentation.Spending 20 minutes stress-testing your assumptions with AI or a simple framework is worth more than 20 hours of word smithing. 

The goal isn't to have a polished PDF to show your mom; it's to find out where your logic is broken before you sink $5k into a dev shop. 

I'm curious—for those of you who actually launched, how much of your original 'plan' survived the first 30 days? Mine was basically trash within a week.

Originally posted here: https://ideatolaunch.co/blog/ai-business-plan-generator-stop-writing-start-validating-mm8y7z58

title: I scraped 50,000+ negative app store reviews. Here are 6 app ideas people are literally begging someone to build:
body:
i got tired of seeing app ideas based on what people think is useful. "wouldn't it be cool if..." cool for who? who's paying?

so i tried something different. scraped over 50,000 negative reviews from the App Store and Google Play Store across hundreds of apps and tracked every pattern where:

\> multiple users across different apps complaining about the same thing

\> existing tools getting dragged in the reviews

\> people saying they'd switch or pay more for something that actually works

\> the same complaint showing up across 5+ competing apps

here are 6 that kept coming up:

a mileage tracker that actually tracks your miles

MileIQ is the biggest name in this space but users keep reporting the same thing: the app randomly stops recording trips. entire months of driving data just gone. one reviewer said "despite having the right settings to track drives, it doesn't come through."

the idea: a mileage tracker with bulletproof auto-detection that runs reliably in the background without users having to babysit it. add manual trip logging as a fallback, automatic QuickBooks and tax software export, and a dashboard that shows exactly which trips were captured and which were missed so nothing slips through. the core value is trust. users need to know every single mile is being logged.

2. a social media scheduler that doesn't break every other week

Planoly is one of the most popular social media planners but the 1 star reviews are brutal. the app crashes constantly, loses scheduled content, and disconnects accounts randomly. one reviewer called it "a nightmare with a pretty color scheme slapped on top." users also report it can't post carousels or reels properly.

the idea: a lightweight social media scheduler built around reliability. rock solid account connections that don't randomly disconnect. full support for every instagram format including carousels, reels, and stories. a visual grid preview that actually loads. and most importantly, scheduled content that posts when it's supposed to. no lost drafts. no mystery failures. just a tool that does what it says.

3. a music learning app that doesn't lock everything after the first lesson

Yousician hooks users with great beginner lessons then walls off 90% of content. "i really liked this app it helped me get going but when it puts 90% behind a paywall why even let the person get going." the built-in tuner is also inaccurate enough that users report it breaking their strings.

the idea: a guitar learning app with a real progressive curriculum that doesn't cut off mid-lesson. include a precision tuner that actually works and won't overtighten strings. let users pick songs they want to learn instead of forcing them through a rigid path. add a practice mode where you can loop specific chord transitions at adjustable speeds. the key differentiator is letting users feel like they're actually progressing, not hitting a wall every 5 minutes.

4. a swim training app that works on every device

Swim Coach has loyal users who credit it with shaving seconds off their personal bests. but the app literally won't open on android after updates. it's not available on Galaxy Watch. and experienced swimmers say the workouts get repetitive.

the idea: a swim training app with full cross-platform support. android, ios, apple watch, galaxy watch, garmin. generate adaptive workouts based on the swimmer's level that actually vary day to day. let users build custom sets with specific rest times and distances. add a logbook to track personal bests over time. sync everything across devices so you can review your workout on your phone right after getting out of the pool.

5. a minimalist goal tracker that lets you organize your goals

Aim has a 4.75 star rating and premium users paying for it. but the number one complaint: you can't reorder your goals. "4 stars, i'll adjust to 5 stars if they add the feature to move goals around."

the idea: a clean, minimalist goal tracker with drag-and-drop reordering, folders or categories to group related goals, and a progress dashboard that shows streaks and completion rates. add customizable reminders via push notifications or sms. let users set milestones within each goal so big targets feel manageable. keep the interface dead simple. no project management bloat. just goals, progress, and the ability to organize them however you want.

6. a QR and barcode scanner that does something useful after the scan

most QR scanners just open a link and that's it. the ones getting 5 star reviews are the ones that add real utility after scanning. "the price comparison feature showed that i could have potentially saved money on some of those purchases. that is real value."

the idea: a QR and barcode scanner that connects to real data after the scan. scan a product barcode and get instant price comparisons across retailers. scan a food item and get nutrition info, allergen warnings, and ingredient breakdowns. scan a business card QR code and auto-create a contact with all their info. scan a receipt and auto-categorize the expense. the scanner itself is commodity. the value is what happens after.

the pattern across all 6:

none of these are sexy. no AI wrappers. no social media tools. they're boring problems where people are already spending money on solutions they hate.

anyway, i got all of these ideas from [a tool](https://bigideasdb.com/app-store-landing) that scrapes and analyzes these reviews automatically across thousands of apps in any specified niche so you don't have to do it manually.

here's the "equation"

specific complaint + existing spend + negative reviews of current tools = someone will pay.

if you're looking for what to build next, stop scrolling and start reading negative reviews. the answers are already there. just copy something and add features users want.

title: What actually makes a product "fundable" from an investor's perspective?
body:
I've been working in product development for about a decade now, and one pattern I see constantly is founders who build amazing prototypes but can't get funded. The product works, the market exists, but investors pass. After being involved in 100+ launches, I've noticed three things that separate fundable products from the ones that stall:

**IP protection matters more than you think.** Investors aren't just buying your product, they're buying defensibility. If someone can copy your idea in three months, you don't have a business moat. Patents, trademarks, and design rights transform an idea into an asset. Look at Dyson, they built an empire on protected cyclone technology. Compare that to fidget spinners, which had zero IP protection. The market flooded with knockoffs instantly, and investors never took it seriously.

**Scalability isn't just about sales projections.** It's about proving your product can go from 100 units to 100,000 without falling apart. Can you manufacture at scale with consistent quality? Do you have distribution channels mapped out? Will your margins improve or get crushed as you grow? Ring didn't just make a doorbell, they built a scalable ecosystem. That's what convinced Amazon to pay a billion dollars.

**Brand positioning creates value.** Even brilliant products die without emotional market connection. Dollar Shave Club sold the most commoditized product imaginable (razors) but positioned as the affordable, convenient alternative. That story built a billion-dollar brand. Generic fitness trackers copied all of Fitbit's features but missed the brand narrative. Investors passed because there was no differentiation worth defending.

The products that raise millions combine all three: they're defensible, scalable, and tell a story that makes people care. If you can't clearly articulate your IP strategy, scalability path, and brand positioning, you're not ready to pitch yet.

The Business Model Idea**:** I want to avoid predatory weekly subscriptions. Instead, I built what I call a "Trust Limiter". The app is completely free, but users are strictly capped at 15 deletes per 24-hour session. If they want to do a massive bulk cleanup and get unlimited swipes, there is a one-time $4.99 Lifetime Pro unlock.

What I want to improve/expand upon**:** I'd love the community's feedback on this approach. Is the strict 15-delete daily limit a good driver for a low-cost $4.99 impulse upgrade, or will it cause immediate day-one churn? How would you improve or combine this idea?

I'm currently in the waitlist stage getting ready to launch. To build some early word-of-mouth and get initial feedback on the core mechanics, I'm giving away the $4.99 Lifetime Pro tier for free to the first 500 waitlist subscribers. We currently have exactly 134 spots left.

title: Built a simple curated video site for my 5-year-old - anyone else tried something similar?
body:
My daughter was falling into the YouTube autoplay rabbit hole for hours, so I put together a basic personal website that only shows videos my wife and I have hand-picked.

We share approved YouTube links in a family chat, tag them by category (education, music, entertainment), and the site pulls them in automatically. No algorithm, no ads, no surprises — just videos we've actually watched and chosen for her age.

A few weeks in and it's genuinely made a difference. She's more engaged, asks better questions, and we feel good about what she's watching.

YouTube Kids was actually our first thought, but we found a couple of issues with it. The interface is very gamified and cartoon-heavy, which kept her just as glued to the screen. The "approved content" feature also still pulls from a massive pool — we wanted something where she can only see videos we have personally watched and decided on ourselves.

The big difference is intentionality. YouTube Kids limits the bad stuff, but our setup means she only sees things we actively chose. There's no browsing, no recommendations, no "up next." She watches what's there and that's it. It also means we can include a real mix — cooking videos, nature documentaries, music performances — things that wouldn't naturally surface together on YouTube Kids.

Has anyone else built something like this or found another way to give kids a curated viewing experience without relying on platform algorithms?

title: Doing to AI Agents what stripe did for online transactions.
body: As most of us know by now, access to large language models have been the sole factor of the modern “AI agent” explosion in many software applications. However, the majority of them by nature are reactive and stateless. Every call starts from zero, which leads to lost context, inconsistent tone, and security risk over time. Most of these agents are just LLM wrappers without persistent identity, which is why my idea is to “humanize” them by building a drop-in LLM proxy that sits between your application and the model. It would be a cognitive structure based on Princeton’s CoALA framework, and could be integrated with only a few lines of code. Features include long-term memory, structured planning, goal alignment, and policy enforcement without requiring you to rebuild your existing workflows. Companies simply route their current AI calls through our runtime, and we transform them into consistent agents that mirror human cognition as opposed to traditional LLM handling. We would also have a GUI interface that allows users to see memory, cognitive mapping, and data storage for each of their cognitive agents.

title: After Working With Multiple Business Owners, This Pattern Keeps Showing Up
body:
One thing I’ve consistently noticed ***working alongside small business owners*** is that most businesses don’t struggle because of bad ideas, **they struggle because growth starts without structure.**

A lot of founders reach a point where **they’re working constantly but results feel inconsistent.** *Marketing becomes trial and error, pricing isn’t fully dialed in, clients come in waves, and every new decision feels heavier than it should.*

The work I spend most of my time on is helping owners move from “figuring things out” into actually operating like a business.

That usually means: • Refining offers and positioning so customers clearly understand the value • Building reliable client acquisition instead of random marketing efforts • Improving engagement and retention so growth compounds • Developing products and services based on real demand signals • Understanding which KPIs actually matter and how to use them • Creating loyalty and repeat-customer systems • identifying operational bottlenecks that slow scaling • Even hiring and going through applications

**Most growth problems** aren’t effort problems *they’re clarity and execution problems*.

**I tend to work best with owners who are already serious about building and understand that sometimes outside perspective is what shortens the learning curve**.

After parting ways with my cofounders, I moved to Berlin to work as an Entrepreneur in Residence at a food delivery company. There, I launched and scaled the catering side, building menus, setting up processes, and closing the first clients, including WeWork, TomTom, and King.

title: I started my HVAC company with $3,200 and never took another dollar in debt. Here’s how.
Don't spend any single penny for Your Website or Saas
body:
If you’re building a **Lovable project**, you can simply migrate it and structure it like this. The key is using **Cloudflare**.

You can run **Next.js on Cloudflare using OpenNext**, which gives you a serverless architecture. Along with that, you can use **Cloudflare R2 for storage**, **D1 for the database**, **Workers for backend logic**, and even **Cloudflare AI** if needed.

Basically, the entire stack is available inside Cloudflare.

The best part is that **you can run everything on the free tier**. Cloudflare provides a very generous free plan for example, you can get **up to 1 million Worker invocations per day for free**.

So you can build and run your SaaS **without paying for hosting in the beginning**

title: Beta Testing a Dating App That Doesn't Care About Your Selfies
body:
Look, we all know how this goes. You spend 20 minutes curating photos, write a bio that's either painfully earnest or desperately trying to be funny, and then what? You match with someone based on whether they liked your hiking pic and now you're stuck making small talk with a stranger you have nothing in common with.

[Cupid's Day Off](https://cupidsdayoff.com) is the opposite of that.

We're building an app that matches you based on behavioral data, not aesthetics. Fill out a personality profile, answer daily scenario questions, and our algorithm figures out who you actually are—not who you're trying to be in six carefully selected photos.

**The mechanics:**

* You answer questions about how you think, react, and make decisions
* We analyze your patterns against actual compatibility markers
* You get matched with people who complement your psychology
* Conversations are encrypted. Photos unlock *after* you confirm mutual interest.

**Why this might not suck:**

* No infinite scroll. We're not trying to keep you on the app.
* No photo-first bias. You can't swipe based on looks alone.
* **It's free for the first 1000 people. Not "free trial" free. Actually free.**
* We're LA-based, so matches are local and you can actually meet people.

**Who we need:**

Cynics. Romantics. People who've deleted every app on their phone at least twice. Anyone who's tired of the "hey" "hi" "how was your weekend?" script. People willing to break shit and tell us when something's broken.

**The ask:**

We're launching in a few weeks. We need beta testers who will actually use the app, answer the questions, and give unfiltered feedback. In return, you get in on the ground floor of something that might actually fix what's broken about dating apps.

*First 1000 users get permanent free access. No upsells. No premium tier bullshit. Just the app, doing what it's supposed to do.*

Comment or DM if you're interested. Or don't. We'll be here either way.

*Cupid's Day Off — behavioral matching for people who think swiping is a waste of time.*

This is the 2026 play.

title: How Much Time Are You Losing on Manual Customer Feedback Analysis?
body: Hey all,We recently realized just how much time our team was losing manually reading feedback. Emails, Slack threads, support tickets, app reviews ,it was taking about 8–12 hours a week just to make sense of it all. That’s basically a full workweek spent trying to understand what our customers were saying.Even after all that, roadmap discussions were still subjective. We’d debate based on the loudest comment instead of recurring issues. The turning point came when we started asking ,How many times has this issue been mentioned in the last 30 days?instead of ,Who said this?Once we tracked recurring themes across channels, the noise dropped. 200 scattered comments turned into 6–8 actionable themes. Automating the clustering process made it even faster, reducing manual sorting and surfacing repeat problems (we use Zefi ai for that).Curious to know ,how much time is your team spending just organizing feedback? Have you actually measured it?

body:

link to last year's post if you want to read it: https://www.reddit.com/r/Startup\_Ideas/s/Gl5mESFMDE

Anyways we finally launched The LockerRoom App last November and have had a good amount of downloads in the couple of months. We started with just the social aspect as it was best to integrate the whole concept of the app at a time.

title: Founders shouldn't have to pay $50/mo just to see if a VC opened their pitch deck. So I had a free alternative built.
body:
I got tired of seeing early-stage founders burn their limited runway on expensive tools like DocSend just to track PDF views. When you are pre-seed, every single dollar matters, and paying just to know if an investor actually opened your deck feels like a shakedown.

I wanted to solve this, so I worked with a developer to build a completely free, secure alternative called OsbornOS. You just upload your deck, it generates a secure link, and it tracks the email of every person who views it. No paywalls, no feature limits.

We just pushed the MVP live today. I’m giving it away for free to the community, but I’d love some brutal feedback from the founders in this sub. What is the #1 feature you think it’s missing right now?

*(Note: I will drop the link to the tool in the comments below so Reddit's spam filters don't delete this post!)*

title: Making it easier to find time to hang out with friends
body:
I know this has probably been proposed or tried many times before, but I still wonder, what are the barriers to people hanging out more other than the obvious (time, schedule, and aligned interests)?

The two ideas:

\- An "impromptu hangout" app that lets you select if you are free and also select which of your friends can see that you are free. You can also select an activity you'd be up for like "food" or "go for a run" or just "free in general". 

\- A "friend calendar" app that shows you when your friends are free so that you can reach out and plan something ahead of time

title: A private local-first “second brain” that organizes and searches inside your files (not just filenames)
body:
[AltDump](http://www.altdump.com/)

 is a simple vault where you drop important files once, and you can search what’s inside them instantly later.



It doesn’t just search filenames. It indexes the actual content inside:

* PDFs
* Screenshots
* Notes
* CSVs
* Code files
* Videos

So instead of remembering what you named a file, you just search what you remember from inside it.

Everything runs locally.  
Nothing is uploaded.
